name: Backend-for-Frontend (BFF)
approvers: Digital Platform Team, Integration Architecture Board
strategicIntent: Provide tailored API backends for each client type (web, mobile, partner portal) to reduce client-side complexity, minimize over-fetching, and limit the API surface area exposed to each consumer.
problemStatement: A single generic API serving web, mobile, and partner consumers forces each client to implement complex data aggregation, filtering, and transformation logic, increasing frontend bundle sizes and exposing unnecessary backend capabilities.

useCases.use: Mobile apps needing optimized payloads to reduce bandwidth on factory floor devices
useCases.dontUse: Internal admin tools with a single web client

useCases.use: Partner portal requiring curated API views with restricted data scope
useCases.dontUse: Service-to-service backend integrations with no end-user client

useCases.use: Progressive web apps needing aggregated responses from multiple microservices
useCases.dontUse: Simple CRUD applications backed by a single database

components: Azure App Service — BFF runtime hosting per client type
Azure API Management — routing layer directing clients to correct BFF
GraphQL or REST aggregation layer — response composition and filtering
Azure AD B2C — client-specific authentication flows
Application Insights — per-BFF performance and error monitoring
interactionSteps: Client (web/mobile/partner) authenticates via Azure AD B2C with client-specific flow
API Management routes request to the appropriate BFF based on client type header
BFF aggregates data from downstream microservices via internal REST/gRPC calls
BFF applies client-specific transformations, filtering, and pagination
Optimized response payload returned to client with cache headers
Application Insights tracks per-BFF latency, error rates, and payload sizes
securityConsiderations: Each BFF must enforce its own OAuth2 scope validation matching client permissions
BFFs must not expose internal microservice schemas to clients
Response filtering must strip fields that the client type is not authorized to receive
Client type identification must use validated JWT claims, not user-agent sniffing
BFF-to-backend calls must use service-to-service credentials, not forwarded user tokens

guardrails.rule: Each distinct client type must have its own BFF instance
BFFs must validate OAuth2 scopes specific to the client type
BFF response payloads must not exceed 500KB for mobile clients
BFF API contracts must be versioned independently of backend services

exceptionProcess: Submit Digital Platform variance request for shared BFF usage across client types. Must document payload analysis showing no over-fetching and security review confirming no scope leakage.

revisionHistory.author: Emma Larsson
revisionHistory.summary: Initial BFF pattern for mobile and web separation

revisionHistory.author: Emma Larsson
revisionHistory.summary: Added partner portal BFF guidance, payload size guardrails, and GraphQL option
